content.problemStatement:
Alice and Bob were playing a game yet again but this time, Bob already lost the game. Now he wants to take revenge.

Bob saw a jigsaw puzzle which was solved by Alice and decided to jumble the puzzle pieces again. There are NNN puzzle pieces in a line numbered from 111 to NNN in increasing order and he has KKK minutes before Alice catches him.

Bob can **swap any two pieces** of the puzzle. Each swap takes 111 minute, and so Bob can make **at most** KKK swaps.

Let AiA_iAi​ denote the piece in the iii-th position after Bob finishes making his swaps.

Alice's angriness is defined to be the number of pairs (i,j)(i, j)(i,j) such that 1≤i<j≤N1 \leq i \lt j \leq N1≤i<j≤N and Ai>AjA_i \gt A_jAi​>Aj​.

Bob would like to make Alice as angry as possible, so he asks for your help: if he performs his swaps optimally, what is the **maximum angriness** he can achieve?


Input Format

The first line of input contains an integer TTT, denoting the number of test cases.
The first and only line of each test case contains two space-separated integers NNN and KKK: the number of pieces and the number of swaps Bob can make.


Output Format

For each test case, output on a new line a single integer: Alice's maximum possible angriness.
content.inputFormat: The first line contains an integer T, the number of test cases. Each of the next T lines contains two space‑separated integers N and K.
content.outputFormat: For each test case output a single line containing the maximum possible angriness (the maximum number of inversions) after at most K swaps.

sampleTestCases.explanation:
Test case 1: N=7, K=3 ≥ floor(7/2)=3, full reverse gives 7·6/2=21 inversions.
Test case 2: N=1, any K gives 0 inversions.
Test case 3: N=8, K=2 < 4, answer = 2·(2·8‑2·2‑1)=2·11=22.
Test case 4: N=5, K=3 ≥ floor(5/2)=2, full reverse gives 5·4/2=10.

solution.approach:
The initial arrangement is [1,2,…,N] (zero inversions). One swap can exchange any two positions. To maximise inversions we want the largest numbers as far left as possible and the smallest numbers as far right as possible. The optimal strategy is:
1. If K ≥ floor(N/2) we can reverse the whole array by swapping symmetric pairs; the number of inversions becomes N·(N‑1)/2.
2. Otherwise we swap the first K elements with the last K elements (i‑th from the left with i‑th from the right). After these K swaps the array looks like:
   [N, N‑1, …, N‑K+1, K+1, K+2, …, N‑K, K, K‑1, …, 1]
   The inversion count can be derived analytically. Let M = N‑2K (the untouched middle part). The contributions are:
   - Inside the first K positions: K·(K‑1)/2
   - Inside the last K positions: K·(K‑1)/2
   - Between first K and middle: K·M
   - Between first K and last K: K·K
   - Between middle and last K: M·K
   Summing gives: K·(2N‑2K‑1).
Thus the answer is:
   if K >= N/2 → N·(N‑1)/2
   else          → K·(2N‑2K‑1)
All calculations fit in 64‑bit signed integers.
The algorithm is O(1) per test case.
solution.timeComplexity: O(T) – constant work for each test case.
